sentences.text: Yes.
My name is Zuzana Adam.
Z-U-Z-A-N-A A-D-A-M.
My maiden name was Klein, K-L-E-I-N.
I was born on June 2, 1925.
I am 70.
I was born in which Miskolc, M-I-S-K-O-L-C, in Hungary.
This is a large city.
It is the second largest city in Hungary, after Budapest.
When I live there before the war, it had 70,000 inhabitants.
But today, it has maybe 200,000.
This is an industrial and commercial city.
My father's name was Vilmos Klein, V-I-L-M-O-S Klein.
Vilmos means William.
He was born also in Miskolc in-- on October 15, 1900.
And my mother's name was Erzsebed.
That's E-R-Z-S-E-B-E-D Klein.
She was born on March 5, 1901, also in Miskolc.
Her maiden name was Weinberger.
That's W-E-I-N-B-E-R-G-E-R.
No, I was an only child.
I was an only child.
Actually, I had a very beautiful and very happy childhood.
My parents, when they were first married, they were very poor, but they both were very, very ambitious, and very skillful people.
My father went to business, and my mother, although her dream was always to be a pediatrician, but of course this couldn't come true.
So she became also a very, very good businesswoman together with my father.
So they managed very well.
So by the time-- what I remember as a child, we were comfortably off.
We were not rich by any means, but very comfortably off, so-- What did your father do?
My father had several things.
He-- he had a grocery store, and he had also a wholesale store for bakery equipment.
And he had also an advertisement agency, but this was mostly my mother's job.
She did the advertisement agency.
Oh, in-- in movies, in theaters, in streetcars, all the different firms, different businesses who advertised with us.
Before the war, there was-- or in the time of the deportation in 1944, there were 14,000 Jews in Miskolc.
Yes.
No, we were by no means orthodox.
My father was more religious than my mother, so whatever my mother did, for instance, a kosher household, she did it because of my father.
But my father went every Saturday to the synagogue.
My mother just went once a year on the High Holy Days.
I was brought up, of course, in Jewish tradition.
This was self-understood.
It couldn't be any other way.
And I had, as I said, a very happy childhood, a very happy youth.
Yeah.
During the war years, the Jews were gradually excluded from all kinds of activities what teenagers do.
So we had our own group.
We had, for instance, a reading club where we invited knowledgeable people, doctors, professors, and so on.
And they had lectures for us.
I remember that this was the place where I first heard about psychoanalysis [LAUGHS], in this club.
And also we were not allowed to go to public swimming pools, for instance.
So we went a little bit outside of the city.
There was a lake, and big group of us boys and girls, we went there.
All Jewish, of course.
And-- and we were still happy.
Only Hungarian, only-- my parents didn't speak any other language, nor my grandparents.
They all were born-- several generations back, they were born in Hungary.
I think it is important to say that Hungarian Jews on the whole were very strongly assimilated, unlike in Czechoslovakia.
They were strongly assimilated.
They spoke Hungarian.
I think just a very small minority who came from Poland or Slovakia-- those people spoke Yiddish.
Otherwise, everybody spoke perfect Hungarian, just like anybody else.
You couldn't tell a Jew apart from a non-Jew because of the language.
Were there Jewish books at home, though?
In-- in Hungarian, but Jewish subjects--
Yes.
Oh yes, oh yes.
There was a Hungarian Jewish weekly.
We subscribed to that, several other Hungarian newspapers.
We had some Jewish books too, and of course, many, many other books.
My mother was a-- [LAUGHS] avid reader.
She always read a great deal, whenever she had time.
So the difficult time started from the 1940s on-- actually, when the war broke out.
Then the first anti-Jewish law was--
Yes.
Oh yes.
My--
Yes, yes.
I remember, for instance, [NON-ENGLISH].
My grandfather used to make the Seder.
Everybody was there.
And my uncle, my mother's brother, he married a Catholic girl.
So they came always to our house for Seder, and usually for Christmas, we went to their house.
So it was a very nice relationship.
There was no animosity between us, really, and it was very nice.
I had another aunt.
They lived in Budapest.
They had one son, my cousin.
They were very often in our house in Miskolc.
And when my aunt died, my cousin was 14 years old, so he came to live with us.
And my mother brought him up to-- up until the end of the war.
From 1938 till '45, till '45.
And we were really like brother and sister.
We always-- even before that, they very often came for a weekend.
His father worked at the railways, and they had free train tickets.
So whenever there was a weekend or a holiday or summer holidays, he was always with us.
So we really grew up together as brother and sister.
I went to a Jewish elementary school.
This was in 1931.
Yes, it had to be, I was six years old.
The Jewish elementary school, four years.
And then, in 1935, I went to a Protestant girls' gymnasium high school.
In Hungary there was no coeducation, so girls' schools were always separate.
Yeah, the Jewish schools as well.
There was a-- it is very difficult to translate in English.
There was a Jewish, like, high school too, but mostly, I think it can be compared like junior high.
So you didn't get a graduation, and of course you couldn't go to university from there.
So I went to this Protestant gymnasium.
In the class where I went, there were 42 children, four of which were Jewish.
Right from the very beginning, we started to suffer from anti-Semitic remarks.
What kind of remarks?
Oh, for instance, I think it was in our first grade in gymnasium, so we were 10 years old.
We were children, really.
It was in the winter.
It was a recess in school, and in the schoolyard we played snowballs.
And one Protestant girl threw a snowball to a Jewish girl.
And naturally, the Jewish girl threw it back.
And this Protestant girl started to yell, you dirty, stinking Jew.
How do you dare to throw a ball at me.
So we four Jewish girls went to our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] homeroom teacher-- it's called homeroom teacher-- and told her this what happened.
And she dismissed us with a remark-- this is our private business, you have to do it among yourselves.
Naturally, with this remark, she opened up the way for all kinds of anti-Semitic remarks and happenings.
They spat.
Spit?
Spit?
Spat at us.
They put their feet out when we went, so we fall.
Tripped you.
We had our religion class in one classroom.
And then they came in, and they said, oh, this is stinking here because Jews were here.
Such and this-- similar remarks.
And naturally, it got worse and worse.
Oh, nearly every day, I went home crying.
It was very, very sad because this was the first time, when I was 10 years old, that I heard anything like this.
I was never exposed to this kind of discrimination.
Not all the teachers were anti-Semitic.
Some were extremely nice, and even later on, during the war years, they were very brave, some of them.
But some of them were just terrible.
They were really fascists.
They were fascist.
So when I was 14 years old-- this was already 1939 when I finished the fourth grade-- this was where the Hitler power, and Hungary was also a half-fascistic state.
So it was clear that I won't be able to go to university because the universities was numerus nullus, not even numerus clausus.
So then there was the question what to do.
To continue the gymnasium, it had no sense.
There was also another option to go to a-- what is it-- commerce school.
But to this I had absolutely no talent, no inclination at all.

At that time, they opened a Jewish girls' school, what was called 'lycee' or 'lyceum.' This gave also a graduation diploma after four years, but you couldn't go to the university with this because we didn't have Latin and several other subjects which they had at the gymnasium.
Nevertheless, I went to this school.
And I went there for three years, till 1942.
In 1942, several things happened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] First of all, my father was taken to a punitive labor camp, to the Ukraine.
My mother was, after her second heart attack.
My grandfather, my maternal grandfather, died.
And when I was 17, I knew already that either I will survive the war, and then I want to go to study at university, or I won't survive the war, and then doesn't matter.
So I went back to the gymnasium and continued privately, which means that I was studying myself at home-- some subjects like, for instance, algebra and physics, I think.
I had a tutor.
And I managed to do the four classes within two years.
At the same time, I could do the household duties because my mother had to be in the store and to earn some living for us.
And I also tutored two children because I wanted to earn the money what I needed for my tutoring.
So this was till '44.
My father came back from the Ukraine in August '43.
So he was there nearly a year and a half.
Why did they take him?
Oh, they took all the Jewish men from very early age, from age maybe 14 or 15, up to 60 or 65.
They took all of them to labor camps at that time, '42.
But in '43, there was a little more liberal government in Hungary.
And they ordered that everybody who was older than 42 must be released and come home.
Luckily, my father was 43.
So he just about made it, and he came home at the end of August 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So at that time, I was still preparing at the last class of the gymnasium.
But on March 19, 1944, the Germans occupied Hungary.
And all the Jewish girls were sent home from the school.
Were they--
So I couldn't-- I couldn't do my graduation exam.
After that, everything went like a very, very fast film.
My father, who was in our city a city alderman, very active in the chamber of commerce and other public activities, as well as in the Jewish community-- he was among the first one who was arrested.
But this was just for a few days, until the Germans somehow accommodated themselves.
But on April 5, if I am not mistaken, 1944, there was already the order to wear the yellow stars.
So we all had to sew on--
Oh yes, oh yes.
I had a raincoat, and on the raincoat my mother sewed on the yellow star.
[LAUGHS] I will never forget.
Once I went on the street in this raincoat and with the star, and across me came one of my classmates, a Protestant girl-- blonde, blue-eyed, suntanned, in a white linen dress.
She was a picture of health and youth and happiness.
Naturally, she didn't-- she didn't greet me, or neither did I her.
But I remember-- then I thought myself, we both are 19, and why should I feel like 100?
I will never forget this picture.
You said, naturally, she didn't greet you.
There was--
Oh, there was a great polarization.
We went to the same class, but there was absolutely no socializing together.
No.
No.
The Jews were together.
My parents had only Jewish friends.
And it was no mixing.
In the main street, in our street where-- in our town, in our city, where there was the what is called the [HUNGARIAN], you know, what is-- a promenade.
One side was for the officers and the Christian community, and on the other side, the Jews promenaded.
[LAUGHS] So it was very, very exactly separated.
That was after the Nazi occupation in March--
Even before that.
Even before that, yes.
We went to a different dance school.
Naturally, teenagers went to dance school and dances.
But this was separate.
There was one for the Christians, and one was for the Jews.
And so--
Big difference from the growing up in total assimilation in society.
Oh yes.
Oh yes.
Oh yes.
Yellow star.
A very short while after that, I think beginning of May, they organized the ghetto in our city.
We lived in a part of the city where there were many Jews, so this part, this district, became the ghetto, which meant--
We stayed, but in our three-room apartment, 54 people were crammed in.
Naturally, all our furniture, my piano, everything went out to the yard, and there were just mattresses put down.
And in our kitchen, some 20 women tried to cook something.
There were small children, babies-- it was hell, really.
It was hell.
Very soon there was-- we heard that it is possible to go to work somewhere.
So I volunteered, and I went to work in a tobacco factory.
This was done in such a way that every morning at 6 o'clock, we had to group in one place in the ghetto.
And then a policeman came, and he took us out from the ghetto to the factory.
And in the evening, he took us back again.
Yes, there were-- no, no.
There was one policeman, one policeman.
So I worked in this factory.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must go back a little bit.
Just a month before the German occupation, on February 19, 1944, I first met my future husband, Jan.
He was teaching Hebrew the two brothers of my girlfriend, and she introduced us.
We went-- this was a very beautiful spring Sunday morning, and we went for a walk.
And I came home, and I told my mother, I met a boy.
He's quite different than anybody else I know.
[LAUGHS] Yeah, so this was the beginning, and then Jan started to come to our house.
And we heard the first time-- we knew that there were deportations from Czechoslovakia already, from 1941, '42.
We knew that and of course what is happening in Poland.
But somehow, the Hungarian Jews were incredibly naive.
And they always, always said the regent, Horthy, won't let us.
Nothing will happen to us.
And they always thought about themselves as Hungarians, so nothing can happen to us.
And Jan told us the stories from his home and from his family.
And he said already then that probably nobody is alive by now.
And we heard from him the first time about the-- about the false papers.
And he said that this is the only way how to save-- how to try at least to save yourself.
Because there were some people who tried to convert.
My father didn't want to hear about that.
Then there were some rumors that they are taking only young girls.
But if the girls are married, they don't take married women.
So there were lots of quick, quick, quick marriages.
And Jan laughed when he heard that.
He said that that's nonsense, nothing like this is going help.
Have papers and try to escape till it is time.
Now, this was very difficult to convince my father because he said I never did anything illegal in my life, and I don't want these papers in my house, and so on and so forth.
My mother was more understanding.
I got the papers, and Jan arranged that.
And my mother had also papers.
I had the name Eva Kriston, E-V-A K-R-I-S-T-O-N.
Hungarian.
Hungarian, of course.
Not just Jewish.
Not just Jewish, yes.
It was-- this person really lived in Miskolc.
She didn't know about it.
She was one year older than I am, so I had to remember that I am one year older.
So I got the papers, and Jan mentioned this already, that he left after the German occupation.
He left Miskolc and went to Budapest.
And in May 26, I went after him to Budapest too.
Before that, we corresponded.
But of course, I couldn't get letters in the ghetto.
So in the tobacco factory, where I worked with two gypsy girls, I took always dresses to them.
I went to work in the morning with several dresses on myself, and then I gave it to them.
And they let me use their address, so Jan wrote the letters to their address, and they brought me Jan's letters.
So we were in contact, and I knew his address in Budapest.
And in May 26, at night, I escaped from the ghetto.
My father knew a police detective, and he gave him some money.
And he came to our house and took me.
And we went to the railway station and with a night train, went to Budapest.
And at the railway station we departed.
I didn't see him anymore.
As I heard from my father later on, just a few days later, he started to blackmail my father.
So much for the nice policeman.
My father was basically against it and didn't believe in it.
My mother was standing in the middle of our apartment, and she said, how can I-- how can I just go?
And what about grandmother?
And she couldn't.
She couldn't.
She hesitated too long, and then it was too late.
May 26 was the last day when I saw her, and also my grandmother.
So I went to Budapest.
From the rail station, I got on a streetcar.
And the first thing was, there was a German, a young German officer, sitting.
And when I stepped in the street car, he jumped up, and with a very, very nice and polite smile, he told me in German, [GERMAN].
Please, miss, have a seat.
And I thought myself, it is about three hours that the yellow star was taken off of my coat, and-- but this was just a little episode.
So I went to the place where [?
Jan ?] was.
And then I looked for a place on my own, of course.
And I found just a bed in a room with a woman, and I stayed there.
At first, of course, I didn't tell her who I am, so she just knew me as Eva, and so on.
I went to work at first to a pastry shop, where on the door there was a sign, 'Gypsies, dogs, and Jews are forbidden to enter,' in that order.
So I worked there.
Maybe the third day, the boss, a woman, called me.
And she told me, you are not smiling enough on the German officers who are coming in here.
Aren't you by any chance Jewish?
So I was very offended, of course, and went home and never went back.
They still owe me my wage.
[LAUGHS]
And then I went to work to a siphon factory or oxygen factory.
We made the siphon glasses.
The--
